text: This video shows a left lower lobectomy performed according to principles of VATS surgery published in Innovations in 2007.
This is a standard three-port VATS procedure using wood retractors for the anterior and posterior port incisions.
As you can appreciate in the video, the anatomy here is extremely favorable and this makes this particular case suitable for teaching.
The first step is dissecting around the lower lobe arterial tributaries and the fissure.
Standard as well as specifically devised instruments can be used to address the sub-adventicial plane.
Once this plane is dissected free, it should be easy to pass a right angle clamp around the artery.
Since for oncological reason, the lower lobe vein needs to be divided first, this structure is addressed at this point.
And to this purpose, the inferior pulmonary ligament is divided with an energy device.
Using endokitness, the vein is deceptive free and a Gonzales reverse clamp is passed cranial to the vein and the vein divided with an endoscopic staple.
Care is taken to articulate the end of stapler perpendicular to the major vein axis.
I usually wait 10 to 15 seconds prior to firing the stapler.

you might have noticed that the usual tie or loop routinely used to suspend the vessel for the end-stapler in this case was not passed since the curved tip of the end-stapler facilitated positioning prior to firing.
By contrast, the previously dissected artery is suspended in order to position the end-stapler which is then fired across both arterial branches to the lower loop at the same time.
The lower lobe bronchus is then skeletonized and dissected, and the endosapler, with a purple reload, is fired.
The final maneuver is to retrieve the specimen with an endoback.
this case the specimen was retrieved through the posterior incision thank you for watching
